questions.question: Explain the difference between strcpy() and strcat() functions with examples.
keyPoints: strcpy() copies one string to another (replaces destination)
strcat() appends one string to another (adds to end)
strcpy(dest, src) - dest must be large enough
strcat(dest, src) - dest must have extra space
Example: strcpy(s1, "Hi"); // s1 becomes "Hi"
Example: strcat(s1, "World"); // s1 becomes "HiWorld"

questions.question: Write a C program to check whether a given character is uppercase, lowercase, or a digit using built-in functions.
keyPoints: Use toupper() and tolower() from ctype.h
Use isupper(), islower(), isdigit() from ctype.h
Include header: #include <ctype.h>
Compare character with functions to determine type
Print appropriate message based on result

questions.question: What is the difference between malloc() and calloc()? Explain with syntax and usage.
keyPoints: malloc() allocates single block of memory
calloc() allocates multiple blocks and initializes to zero
malloc(n) vs calloc(n, size)
calloc: (int*)calloc(5, sizeof(int)) - 5 integers initialized to 0
malloc: (int*)malloc(5*sizeof(int)) - contains garbage values
calloc takes longer due to initialization

questions.question: Explain isalpha(), isdigit(), and isalnum() functions with example code snippet.
keyPoints: isalpha() - returns true for A-Z or a-z
isdigit() - returns true for 0-9
isalnum() - returns true for letters or digits
All from ctype.h header file
Return non-zero for true, zero for false
Example: isalpha('A') returns non-zero, isdigit('5') returns non-zero

questions.question: What is the purpose of srand() function? How is it used with rand() to generate random numbers?
keyPoints: srand() seeds the random number generator
rand() generates pseudo-random numbers
Without srand(), same sequence every run
srand(time(NULL)) uses current time as seed
time(NULL) from time.h returns seconds since epoch
Common pattern: srand(time(NULL)); num = rand() % range;

questions.question: Differentiate between abs() and fabs() functions in C with examples.
keyPoints: abs() is for integer absolute value
fabs() is for floating-point absolute value
abs() returns int, defined in stdlib.h
fabs() returns double, defined in math.h
Example: abs(-5) returns 5
Example: fabs(-5.5) returns 5.5

questions.question: Write a complete C program that: (a) Uses malloc() to allocate memory for an array of 10 integers (b) Accepts 10 numbers from user and stores them (c) Calculates and displays the sum and average (d) Frees the allocated memory using free().
keyPoints: Include required headers: stdio.h, stdlib.h
Use malloc: int *arr = (int*)malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
Check if malloc returns NULL (failed allocation)
Use loop to accept 10 numbers with scanf()
Calculate sum using loop, average = sum / 10.0
Display results with printf()
Free memory: free(arr); arr = NULL; (good practice)

questions.question: Write a C program that uses string built-in functions to: (a) Accept two strings from user (b) Compare them using strcmp() and display which is greater (c) Concatenate them using strcat() (d) Find length of result using strlen().
keyPoints: Include string.h header file
Declare character arrays with sufficient size
Use gets() or fgets() for string input
strcmp() returns 0 if equal, negative if first<string, positive if first>string
strcat() appends second string to first (ensure enough space)
strlen() returns number of characters excluding null terminator
Display results appropriately

questions.question: Write a C program to generate 10 random numbers between 1 and 100. Explain the use of srand() and time() functions in this context.
keyPoints: Include stdlib.h and time.h headers
Call srand(time(NULL)) to seed random generator
Use rand() % 100 + 1 to get 1-100 range
Use loop to generate 10 numbers
time(NULL) returns seconds since epoch (Unix)
Same seed produces same sequence, time() ensures different each run
Print each random number with proper formatting

questions.question: Create a menu-driven C program that performs various operations using built-in functions: (a) String length using strlen() (b) String copy using strcpy() (c) String comparison using strcmp() (d) Character classification using isalpha(), isdigit().
keyPoints: Create switch-case menu system
For strlen: pass string, display length
For strcpy: get source string, copy to destination, display
For strcmp: get two strings, compare, display result
For character functions: get character, test with isalpha/isdigit
Include all necessary headers: stdio.h, string.h, ctype.h
Use appropriate input functions and display results
